ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general almond milk entry; flavoured/sweetened variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers almond milk generically; flavoured variant not separately enumerated. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured almond milk as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of flavoured almond milk is one glass of approximately 240 ml (240 g), as commonly consumed as a beverage or poured over cereal.

nutrient_source.title: Almond milk, sweetened, vanilla flavour — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14639 and branded equivalents)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo record for flavoured almond milk (cr_id F01020003, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for sweetened vanilla almond milk (FDC ID 1097549), which reports approximately 0.07 g saturated fat per 100 g. Calcium and iron are reported as 0.0 mg in the HPB source for this unfortified flavoured variant; confirmed zero per HPB. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrients not reported in either source and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk and plant-based milk alternatives — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for flavoured almond milk was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with unsweetened almond milk (GI ~25, University of Sydney GI Database) and sweetened plant-based milks. The added sugar (6.87 g/100 g, predominantly sucrose/glucose) raises effective GI slightly above unsweetened almond milk but the low carbohydrate matrix and fat content keep the overall GI in the low range. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 17.5) / 100 = 4.4. Serving size used: 240 g × 7.29 g carbohydrate/100 g = 17.5 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage classification: 6.87 g sugar per 100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; sweetened beverages independently raise blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines regardless of moderate sodium content.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid flavoured almond milk and choose unsweetened almond milk (0 g added sugar) instead, as the 6.87 g/100 g sugar content classifies this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which independently elevates blood pressure risk independent of its sodium level of 83 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (6.87 g/100 g) in a liquid matrix with dietary fibre <1 g/100 g and minimal protein (0.41 g/100 g) results in rapid glucose absorption and postprandial blood glucose elevation without fibre or protein modulation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid flavoured almond milk due to its 6.87 g/100 g sugar content in a low-fibre (0.2 g/100 g), low-protein (0.41 g/100 g) liquid matrix that promotes rapid postprandial glucose spikes; unsweetened almond milk is the recommended substitute.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar at 6.87 g/100 g in a beverage format contributes to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly; fat content is low (1.04 g/100 g) and saturated fat is negligible (0.07 g/100 g), which is favourable, but the sugar load offsets this benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, flavoured almond milk should be consumed with caution or replaced with unsweetened almond milk, as the 6.87 g/100 g added sugar content can raise triglycerides and contribute to cardiovascular risk with habitual consumption, despite its low saturated fat profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar with fibre <1 g/100 g and absence of meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, negligible vitamin E in flavoured commercial variant) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid flavoured almond milk and choose unsweetened, vitamin-E-retaining almond milk, as the added sugar content promotes inflammatory mediators without any compensating anti-inflammatory nutritional benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured almond milk is a commercially produced, dairy-free, plant-based beverage made from ground almonds blended with water, to which flavouring agents and added sugars are incorporated during manufacturing. Common flavour variants include vanilla, chocolate, and honey. The product is ultra-processed and typically sold in shelf-stable cartons or refrigerated formats. Its texture is thin and watery compared to dairy milk, with a mildly sweet, nutty taste that varies by flavour variant. Per 100 g, it provides 40 kcal, 0.41 g protein, 1.04 g fat, 7.29 g carbohydrate, 6.87 g sugar, 0.2 g dietary fibre, and 83 mg sodium. The high sugar-to-fibre ratio (34:1) and liquid matrix classify this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under AHA 2018 and WHO dietary guidelines. Unlike unsweetened almond milk, the flavoured variant provides no meaningful calcium or iron in this unfortified HPB-sourced formulation (both reported as 0.0 mg/100 g). Clinically, this product is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its SSB classification and rapid glucose absorption profile. It is suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals and vegans as a low-calorie dairy substitute, provided the sugar content is accounted for in total daily intake. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 25 (low) by analogy with unsweetened almond milk, but the glycemic load per 240 ml serving is 4.4, which is clinically meaningful in the context of multiple daily servings.
common_user_queries: Is flavoured almond milk good for diabetics?
Can I drink almond milk if I have high blood pressure?
How much sugar is in flavoured almond milk?
What is the glycemic index of almond milk?
Is almond milk good for weight loss?
Can people with lactose intolerance drink almond milk?
What is the difference between flavoured and unsweetened almond milk?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F01020003 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central sweetened almond milk entry (FDC ID 1097549). GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database for almond milk. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific flavoured almond milk formulation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this exact product as of 2026-04-16. Calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 mg per 100 g: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source for this unfortified flavoured almond milk variant (cr_id F01020003); this product does not appear to be calcium- or iron-fortified in the HPB formulation. Note that many commercial flavoured almond milks are calcium-fortified; consumers should check product labels. Saturated_fat_g of 0.07 g/100 g is adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 1097549 (Almond milk, sweetened, vanilla flavour) as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for unsweetened almond milk; no direct GI measurement for flavoured almond milk was identified in approved sources. The dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input are retained as contextually accurate (sodium 83 mg/100 g is moderate-low; energy 40 kcal/100 g is low-calorie), but 'low_sodium' should be interpreted with caution given SSB classification. The tag 'plant_based' is accurate.
